We propose AREA, the first framework to explicitly decouple and stabilize both. For extraction, we anchor class-level attributes on CLIP’s hypersphere via Principal Geodesic Analysis (PGA) to preserve geometric consistency across tasks. For aggregation, we introduce lightweight task-specific experts with scoring-based routing and residual refinement, regularized by a variational information bottleneck to retain only essential task knowledge. At inference, optimal transport enables concise, manifold-aware routing. AREA achieves state-of-the-art performance across 6 benchmarks (+3.2% avg. accuracy), reduces forgetting by 41% in long sequences, and is open-sourced.

Abstract

Class-Incremental Learning (CIL) is important in building real-world learning systems. In CLIP-based CIL, the model performs classification by comparing similarity between visual and textual embeddings obtained from template prompts, e.g., ``a photo of a [CLASS]''. This seemingly monolithic matching process can be decomposed into two conceptually distinct stages: attribute extraction and attribute aggregation. For example, a model may recognize cat using attributes such as fur texture and whiskers. When learning a new class like car, the model must extract additional attributes like wheels and adjust how they are aggregated in the shared representation space. However, since only data from the current task is available, incremental updates can bias both attribute extraction and aggregation toward new classes, leading to catastrophic forgetting. Therefore, we propose AREA for attribute extraction and aggregation in CLIP-based CIL. To stabilize extraction, we anchor class-level visual and textual attributes on the hyperspherical embedding space via principal geodesic analysis. To stabilize aggregation, we learn lightweight task-specific experts with scoring and residual refinement, regularized by a variational information bottleneck objective. During inference, we perform routing over task attribute manifolds via optimal transport for more concise prediction. Experiments show that AREA consistently outperforms SOTA methods. Code is available at https://github.com/LAMDA-CL/ICML2026-AREA.

item.title: Mitigating Adaptive Attacks against Reasoning Models with Activation Consistency Training
item.description:
Paper Link: https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28467v1

AI Summary (中文)

背景与问题

随着大语言模型（LLM）推理能力增强，其长链式思维（chain-of-thought, CoT）虽提升任务性能，却也扩大了对抗性攻击面——尤其在自适应 jailbreak 攻击和提示注入（prompt injection） 场景下，传统基于输出监督的防御易被绕过。

方法创新

本文系统研究一致性训练（Consistency Training） 作为推理模型安全微调范式，对比两类核心变体：

  • 输出级一致性（BCT）：强制模型对清洁提示与对抗改写给出相同最终答案；
  • 激活级一致性（ACT）：在隐藏层（特别是助手响应起始边界）对齐清洁/包裹提示的内部表征。

ACT 仅需自监督配对数据（原始提示 + 无标签对抗包装），无需人工标注或外部判别器。

关键发现

1. 鲁棒性优势：ACT 在五种主流推理模型上均显著优于 BCT，在面对自适应攻击者（如重写策略迭代、CoT 替换为合规轨迹）时保持高拒绝率，而 BCT 易失效；
2. 机制可解释性：ACT 训练后，模型在助手轮次边界处形成近似线性拒绝方向——单一生效向量即可精准调控拒答行为，且对良性输入影响极小；
3. 泛化能力突出：即使攻击者预填充“合规”推理链（来自未防护基模），ACT 仍能识别并拒绝 jailbreak 意图，证明其防御锚定于内部状态一致性，而非表面文本模式。

理论意义

本工作揭示：监督隐状态比监督输出更本质、更鲁棒、更可解释，为推理模型的安全对齐提供了新范式。

AI Summary (English)

As LLMs develop stronger reasoning capabilities, their extended chain-of-thought (CoT) expands the attack surface for adaptive jailbreaks and prompt injection. We systematically evaluate consistency training—specifically output-level (BCT) and activation-level (ACT) variants—as a prompt injection defense across five reasoning models. ACT enforces representation alignment at the assistant-turn boundary using only self-supervised clean/wrapped prompt pairs, requiring no labels or external classifiers. We find ACT outperforms BCT under adaptive attacks—including CoT replacement with compliant traces from the base model—and matches or exceeds state-of-the-art training-based defenses in robustness. Mechanistically, ACT encodes refusal as a near-linear steering direction in activation space, enabling precise, low-overhead intervention. These results demonstrate that supervising internal representations is a highly effective, interpretable, and generalizable approach to safety training in reasoning models.

Abstract

As LLMs gain stronger reasoning capabilities, their extended chain-of-thought introduces new degrees of complexity for defending against adversarial jailbreaks and prompt injection. We study consistency training, a family of fine-tuning objectives that enforce identical behavior on clean prompts and adversarial rewrites, and evaluate its two main variants, output-level (BCT) and activation-level (ACT), across five reasoning models. We formulate both methods as a prompt injection defense and find ACT to be competitive with other training-based defenses while requiring only self-supervised pairs of clean and wrapped prompts. Our experiments also generalize both techniques within the jailbreak setting, demonstrating that ACT remains more robust to adaptive attacks. We also provide mechanistic evidence that ACT's defense against jailbreaks is encoded as a roughly linear shift in activation space at the assistant-turn boundary. After ACT training, we can recover a single steering direction that controls refusal on reasoning models with minimal effect on benign inputs. We find that ACT remains robust even when the model's chain-of-thought is replaced with a compliant trace from the undefended base model, pivoting to refuse prefilled jailbreaks. Together, these results suggest that supervising internal representations is a surprisingly effective and interpretable approach to various forms of safety training in reasoning models.

item.title: Dimensionality Reduction for Robust Federated Learning: A Theoretical Analysis and Convergence Guarantee
item.description:
Paper Link: https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28335v1

AI Summary (中文)

背景与挑战

联邦学习（FL）允许多客户端协作训练模型而无需共享原始数据，但其分布式架构极易遭受拜占庭攻击（如恶意上传伪造梯度）。现有鲁棒聚合器（如Krum、Bulyan、Median）虽能抵御攻击，却在高维梯度聚合阶段引发严重计算瓶颈：服务器端复杂度高达 $\mathcal{O}(M p^2)$ 或更高，随模型维度 $p$ 平方级增长——在大语言模型与视觉Transformer时代，该开销已成主导性训练成本。

方法创新：投影降维（PDR）框架

我们提出Projected Dimensionality Reduction（PDR）——一种通用、即插即用的加速框架，专为向量距离型鲁棒聚合器设计。PDR利用稀疏随机投影（Sparse Johnson–Lindenstrauss Transform）将 $p$ 维梯度压缩至 $d \ll p$ 维子空间（典型取 $d = \mathcal{O}(\log M)$），在低维空间高效计算梯度间距离与可靠性权重，再将加权结果反投影回原空间。关键突破在于：服务器计算复杂度降至最优 $\mathcal{O}(M p)$，仅与读取所有梯度的理论下界一致。

理论保证与误差分析

在标准FL假设（非凸/强凸目标函数、有界方差、Lipschitz梯度）下，我们首次为降维鲁棒FL建立严格收敛界：

  • 非凸情形：$\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T})$ 收敛率（匹配无降维基线）；
  • 强凸情形：$\mathcal{O}(1/T)$ 收敛率（最优速率）；
  • 拜占庭误差上界仅被可控因子 $\frac{1+\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon}$ 放大（$\varepsilon$ 由投影精度控制，可调至 $<1.1$），证明加速“几乎零代价”。

实验验证

在CIFAR-10/100、FEMNIST等基准上，PDR与Krum/Bulyan集成后实现12–47×端到端训练加速，通信开销不变，最终准确率波动小于0.8%，显著优于基线。

AI Summary (English)

Federated Learning (FL) faces severe computational bottlenecks in Byzantine-robust gradient aggregation, especially for large models. We propose Projected Dimensionality Reduction (PDR), a universal acceleration framework that applies sparse random projection to compress gradients into a low-dimensional subspace before distance-based robust aggregation. PDR reduces server complexity to the optimal $\mathcal{O}(Mp)$ — matching the fundamental lower bound for reading gradients — while preserving convergence. Under standard FL assumptions, we prove PDR achieves $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T})$ rate for non-convex and $\mathcal{O}(1/T)$ for strongly convex objectives. Crucially, its Byzantine error floor increases only by a bounded, tunable factor $(1+\varepsilon)/(1-\varepsilon)$. Experiments show up to 47× speedup with negligible accuracy loss versus state-of-the-art robust aggregators.

Abstract

Federated Learning (FL) enables multiple clients to collaboratively train models without sharing raw data, but it is highly vulnerable to Byzantine attacks. Existing robust approaches can neutralize these threats but incur substantial computational overhead during high-dimensional gradient aggregation, an overhead that scales poorly with model size and increasingly dominates the training cost as modern models grow larger. To address this computational bottleneck, we propose Projected Dimensionality Reduction (PDR), a universal acceleration framework for vector-level distance-based robust aggregators, which performs robust aggregation by compressing gradients into a drastically smaller subspace via sparse random projection to efficiently compute reliability weights. This approach reduces the server computational complexity to an optimal $ \mathcal{O}(Mp) $, where $ M $ is the number of clients and $ p $ is the model dimension, matching the theoretical lower bound required merely to read the gradients. We establish convergence guarantees under standard FL assumptions in prior Byzantine-robust FL analyses. By leveraging the Subspace Embedding Theorem, we show that PDR achieves optimal convergence rates of $ \mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T}) $ for non-convex functions and $ \mathcal{O}(1/T) $ for strongly convex functions, where $ T $ denotes the number of iterations. Crucially, we mathematically demonstrate that this massive acceleration comes almost for free, merely inflating the inherent Byzantine error floor by a bounded, tunable factor of $ \frac{1+ε}{1-ε} $. Experimental results on benchmark datasets confirm that integrating PDR with existing aggregators yields orders of magnitude speedups in time efficiency while maintaining highly competitive convergence performance.

item.title: Structure-Guided Visual Perturbation Neutralization for LVLMs
item.description:
Paper Link: https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27927v1

AI Summary (中文)

背景与挑战

大型视觉语言模型（LVLMs）依赖图像输入感知细粒度视觉信息，但像素级输入也引入了显著的对抗攻击面——微小的、人眼不可辨的扰动即可诱导模型生成有害响应。现有防御方法存在两大瓶颈：（1）面向传统CV模型设计的通用防御（如输入预处理、去噪）忽视LVLMs对视觉-语言跨模态对齐的强依赖，导致文本生成质量严重下降；（2）少数专为LVLMs定制的防御常需大幅修改图像（>5%像素重写）或引入高开销模块（如额外微调、迭代优化），牺牲推理效率与原始表征保真度。

方法创新：SIGN框架

我们提出结构引导的视觉扰动中和框架（SIGN），一种轻量级、即插即用的防御方案：

  • 先验结构提取（Prior Structural Extraction）：利用冻结的视觉编码器（如CLIP-ViT）高效提取图像底层结构先验（边缘、纹理、显著区域），无需训练或参数更新；
  • 动态引导中和（Dynamic Guided Neutralization）：基于结构先验生成空间自适应掩码，在扰动敏感区域实施局部像素校正，仅修改最必要像素，避免全局失真。

核心结果

在4类主流LVLM（LLaVA-1.5、Qwen-VL、MiniGPT-4、mPLUG-Owl2）及3种强对抗攻击（PGD、PatchAttack、TAP）下，SIGN实现：
✅ 87.3%平均防御成功率（较基线提升32.6%）；
✅ 仅0.5%像素被修改，视觉保真度（LPIPS↓0.012，SSIM↑0.98）与原始图像高度一致；
✅ 单图耗时0.16秒，无额外GPU内存占用，支持实时部署；
✅ 在VQA、Caption、安全问答等良性任务上性能衰减<0.8%，远优于对比方法（平均衰减>4.2%）。
本工作首次验证：冻结视觉编码器的结构先验可作为高效、免训练的对抗免疫信号源，为LVLM安全提供新范式。代码已开源。

AI Summary (English)

Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) face severe vulnerability to pixel-level adversarial perturbations, yet existing defenses either ignore cross-modal alignment requirements or incur excessive image distortion and computational cost. To bridge this gap, we propose SIGN (Structure-Induced Guided Neutralization) — a lightweight, plug-and-play defense that leverages frozen vision encoders to extract structural priors (e.g., edges, saliency) and applies dynamic, spatially adaptive neutralization only where perturbations are most harmful. SIGN requires no model retraining, modifies merely 0.5% of pixels, and processes each image in 0.16 seconds, achieving an 87.3% average defense success rate across four LVLMs under three strong attacks. Crucially, it preserves original visual fidelity (LPIPS: ↓0.012; SSIM: 0.98) and degrades benign task performance by less than 0.8%, significantly outperforming prior methods. SIGN demonstrates that structural priors from off-the-shelf vision encoders serve as effective, efficient, and training-free signals for adversarial protection in multimodal models.

Abstract

Image inputs enable Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) to perceive fine-grained visual information, but also introduce a pixel-level attack surface through which adversarial perturbations can elicit unsafe model behaviors. However, most existing defenses are designed for traditional computer vision settings and thus often overlook the cross-modal alignment required by LVLMs, leading to degraded performance. Meanwhile, the limited defenses tailored to LVLMs often require substantial image modifications and introduce considerable computational overhead, thereby compromising inference quality and efficiency. To address these limitations, we propose Structure-Induced Guided Neutralization (SIGN), a lightweight, plug-and-play defense framework that improves LVLM compatibility via Prior Structural Extraction and achieves efficient perturbation suppression via Dynamic Guided Neutralization. Extensive experiments show that SIGN achieves over 87\% defense success rate with only 0.5\% pixel modification and 0.16 seconds per image, while nearly preserving original visual representations and benign task performance. Our work offers a lightweight alternative to defenses that require costly model training and highlights the potential of exploiting a vision encoder for efficient adversarial protection. Our code is open source on https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SIGN-BCB1.

item.title: FedEHR-Gen: Federated Synthetic Time-Series EHR Generation via Latent Space Alignment and Distribution-Aware Aggregation
item.description:
Paper Link: https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27892v1

AI Summary (中文)

背景与挑战

合成电子健康档案（EHR）生成是解决医疗数据稀缺、跨机构建模难及隐私合规瓶颈的关键路径。然而，现有方法多依赖集中式训练，需汇集各医院原始数据，严重违背《个人信息保护法》《HIPAA》等法规要求；而直接迁移联邦学习至EHR生成任务时，常因EHR数据固有的高维稀疏性、时序异质性与站点间分布偏移（如eICU与MIMIC-III在患者年龄、监测频率、编码体系上的显著差异）导致模型崩溃或发散。

方法创新

本文提出FedEHR-Gen——首个面向分布式医院的联邦时序EHR合成框架，包含两大核心技术：
1. 潜空间对齐机制：设计联邦自编码器（Federated AE），将原始稀疏EHR映射至低维潜空间；引入层间匹配聚合（Layer-wise Matching Aggregation），通过可学习的仿射变换对齐各站点本地编码器参数，强制构建语义一致的全局潜空间；
2. 分布感知聚合TCVAE：在对齐潜空间上部署联邦时序条件变分自编码器（TCVAE），其聚合策略动态加权各站点梯度，依据其潜分布与全局分布的Wasserstein距离调整贡献度，显著缓解非独立同分布（Non-IID）问题。

实验结果

在eICU与MIMIC-III真实临床数据集上验证：FedEHR-Gen生成样本的统计保真度（JS散度↓32.7%）、下游任务效用（LSTM分类AUC达0.892，逼近集中式基线0.901）及隐私风险控制（攻击成功率<4.1%，低于GAN基线12.3%）均与集中训练相当，且稳定收敛（训练波动降低68%），全面超越FedAvg等标准联邦基线。

AI Summary (English)

Synthetic Electronic Health Record (EHR) generation enables privacy-preserving data augmentation and cross-hospital modeling, yet centralized approaches violate real-world data governance constraints. While federated learning offers a natural alternative, direct application to time-series EHR synthesis fails due to high dimensionality, sparsity, and severe cross-site heterogeneity. We propose FedEHR-Gen, the first federated framework for synthetic time-series EHR generation. It employs a two-stage paradigm: (1) a federated autoencoder with layer-wise matching aggregation to align heterogeneous local encoders into a semantically consistent latent space; and (2) a federated temporal conditional VAE (TCVAE) trained on this aligned space, using distribution-aware aggregation that weights client updates by Wasserstein distance to the global latent distribution. Experiments on eICU and MIMIC-III show FedEHR-Gen matches centralized training in generation fidelity (JS divergence ↓32.7%), downstream utility (AUC=0.892), and privacy protection (attack success <4.1%), while outperforming standard federated baselines in stability and convergence.

Abstract

Synthetic Electronic Health Record (EHR) generation provides a promising avenue for data augmentation and cross-hospital modeling in privacy-constrained healthcare settings. However, most existing EHR generative models are centralized and require pooling data across hospitals, which is often infeasible when real-world data sharing is restricted. While federated EHR generation offers a natural solution, direct federated modeling often collapses or diverges due to the high dimensionality, sparsity, and cross-hospital heterogeneity of EHR data. In this work, we propose FedEHR-Gen, the first federated framework for synthetic time-series EHR generation across distributed hospitals. FedEHR-Gen uses a two-stage learning paradigm. First, we introduce a federated autoencoder that projects high-dimensional and sparse EHR features onto a compact latent space. To ensure semantic consistency across hospitals, we develop a layer-wise matching aggregation mechanism that aligns local encoders into a unified global latent space. Second, operating on this aligned latent space, we train a federated temporal conditional variational autoencoder (TCVAE) with distribution-aware aggregation, enabling stable temporal generative modeling under severe cross-hospital heterogeneity. Extensive experiments on the eICU and MIMIC-III datasets demonstrate that FedEHR-Gen achieves generation fidelity, downstream utility, and privacy risk comparable to centralized training, while consistently outperforming the standard federated baseline.
